name: Kami of Fire
description: The Kami of Fire, often referred to as Kagutsuchi (also known as Homusubi), is a powerful and complex figure in Japanese mythology. Born from the goddess Izanami during her creation of the Japanese islands with her husband Izanagi, Kagutsuchi's birth tragically resulted in Izanami's death from the severe burns inflicted upon her. This event is considered one of the most significant in Japanese mythology, as it led to Izanagi's descent into Yomi, the underworld, and the subsequent creation of other important deities. Kagutsuchi embodies the raw, untamed power of fire, representing both its destructive and creative aspects. He is associated with blacksmithing, volcanoes, and the hearth, symbolizing both the potential for destruction and the vital warmth and light that fire provides. While often depicted as a fearsome and dangerous deity due to his role in Izanami's death, Kagutsuchi is also revered for his essential role in the creation of tools and weapons, and for the warmth and protection he offers. Temples and shrines dedicated to fire-related deities often implicitly or explicitly honor Kagutsuchi, recognizing his fundamental position within the Shinto pantheon. He is a reminder of the delicate balance between creation and destruction, a concept central to Shinto beliefs.
subtitle: The embodiment of fire's duality.

keyMyths.title: Birth of Kagutsuchi
keyMyths.description: Kagutsuchi's birth burns Izanami, leading to her death and Izanagi's descent into Yomi.

keyMyths.title: Izanagi's Grief
keyMyths.description: Wracked with grief, Izanagi slays Kagutsuchi, creating other deities from the blood.

keyMyths.title: Association with Forges
keyMyths.description: Kagutsuchi's fire is linked to the creation of metals and the art of blacksmithing.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An annotated translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle.
sources.text: An annotated translation of the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle.

sources.author: W.G. Aston

sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another foundational text of Japanese mythology.
